title: What is a pointer?
instruction:
A pointer holds a **memory address** — the location of another variable.

- `int* p` — pointer to int
- `&x` — address of x (address-of operator)
- `*p` — value at the address (dereference operator)

```cpp
int x = 42;
int* p = &x;   // p holds the address of x
*p = 99;       // change x through the pointer
```

Run this.
successMessage: p and &x print the same memory address. *p = 99 changed x through the pointer. The address will be different every run — the OS puts variables wherever it wants. Pointer manipulation is how memory works.

title: nullptr — the safe null pointer
instruction:
A pointer that points to nothing should be set to `nullptr` (C++11).

Dereferencing a null pointer is **undefined behaviour** — your program might crash, corrupt data, or worse.

Always check for nullptr before dereferencing.
successMessage: Always null-check before dereferencing. nullptr is safer than the old NULL or 0 — it's type-safe and the compiler can catch misuse. Most segmentation faults are null pointer dereferences.

title: Pointer arithmetic
instruction:
You can do arithmetic on pointers — adding 1 moves to the NEXT element of that type:

```cpp
int arr[] = {10, 20, 30};
int* p = arr;  // points to arr[0]
p++;           // now points to arr[1]
```

This is how C arrays and low-level memory access work.
successMessage: p+1 moves to the next int (4 bytes forward). This is how vector iterators are implemented internally. Raw pointer iteration is how the original STL algorithms worked before range-based for.

title: const with pointers
instruction:
Two kinds of const with pointers:

```cpp
const int* p = &x;  // pointer to const int — can't change *p
int* const p = &x;  // const pointer — can't change p (where it points)
const int* const p; // both are const
```

Read from right to left: `const int* p` = "p is a pointer to const int".
successMessage: const int* p — the value is const, the pointer can move. int* const p — the pointer is const, the value can change. Function parameters almost always use const T* or const T& — read-only access without copying.

title: References vs pointers
instruction:
References are safer, simpler aliases:

| Feature | Pointer | Reference |
|---------|---------|----------|
| Null? | Yes (nullptr) | Never null |
| Reassign? | Yes | No — always same object |
| Syntax | `*p`, `p->` | `r`, `.` |
| Use for | Optional/arrays | Parameters |

Rule: use references when you can, pointers when you must.
successMessage: References never need null checks — they're always valid. ref = 100 changes x because ref IS x. Use references for parameters; use pointers for optional values, arrays, and raw memory access.

title: Pointers to strings and arrays
instruction: Array names are pointers to their first element. C-strings are `const char*` — a pointer to the first character.
successMessage: Arrays decay to pointers when passed to functions. C-strings are null-terminated char arrays. cstr + 7 moves 7 characters forward. This is how C worked before C++ — understanding it makes you a better programmer.

title: Build a pointer toolkit
instruction:
No starter code.

Write three functions using pointers:
1. `void swapValues(int* a, int* b)` — swap two integers via pointers
2. `int* findMax(int* arr, int size)` — return pointer to the largest element
3. `void fillArray(int* arr, int size, int value)` — fill array with a value

Test each.
hint: swapValues: int temp = *a; *a = *b; *b = temp;\nfindMax: int* maxPtr = arr; for loop comparing *ptr > *maxPtr

codeChecks.message: Define void swapValues(int* a, int* b).
Define int* findMax(int* arr, int size).
Define void fillArray(int* arr, int size, int value).

successMessage: Pointer-based utilities — the foundation of every C++ data structure. Swap via pointers is the simplest pointer exercise; findMax returning a pointer lets the caller modify the max value. Chapter 9 done!
